title: 50 Years Behind the Camera: What Media Insiders Don't Want You to Know
guestBio: Steve Edwards is a legendary Los Angeles broadcaster with over 50 years in television, including hosting Good Day LA for 17 years. He has received the Governor's Award from the Academy of Television Arts & Sciences and has a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
excerpt: What does five decades inside the media machine teach you that they'll never say on air? Legendary broadcaster Steve Edwards pulls back the curtain on the hidden truths of media, the power of gratitude, and why authenticity always wins.
metaDescription: Legendary broadcaster Steve Edwards reveals 50 years of media industry secrets, career longevity tips, and why gratitude is your superpower.
keyTakeaways: Gratitude is a daily superpower that can combat negative mental spirals and transform your perspective on challenges.
Career longevity requires constant reinvention while staying true to your authentic self rather than performing a character.
Social media is having unprecedented negative impacts on young people, particularly girls, that we haven't fully grasped yet.
The internet and cable news have accelerated tribalism in ways our society hasn't caught up to, fragmenting our shared reality.
Authenticity beats performance every single time, whether you're on camera or in any leadership role.
Staying relevant in any career means never stopping your curiosity and willingness to learn and adapt.
Breaking news coverage requires balancing truth-telling with responsibility for the audience's emotional wellbeing.

pullQuotes.text: Gratitude will get you through anything - it's a daily practice that can change your entire perspective
Be yourself - the best version of yourself. Authenticity beats performance every single time
We have more information than ever before, but less wisdom about how to use it

timestamps.label: The Role of Morning Shows in Society
The Hollywood Walk of Fame Moment
The Walter Cronkite Era vs. Today
Social Media's Impact on Young Girls

content:
What happens when someone who spent five decades behind the camera finally decides to tell the truth about what really goes on? You get an episode like this one with Steve Edwards, the legendary Los Angeles broadcaster who managed to build a 50-year career without ever getting fired. And trust me, what he reveals will change how you think about media, career longevity, and the power of staying authentic in a world that rewards performance.

Steve's journey from clinical psychology student to morning show icon wasn't planned, but it teaches us something crucial about career success: curiosity and authenticity will take you further than any strategic plan ever could. When he stumbled into broadcasting in 1967, he brought something rare to the industry—genuine curiosity about people and an unwillingness to perform a character on air.

The Hidden Truth About Media Tribalism

Here's what Steve reveals that should make every leader pay attention: the media landscape we're navigating today isn't just different—it's fundamentally broken in ways that affect how we communicate, lead, and connect with others. The rise of cable news and social media has accelerated tribalism to a degree our society hasn't caught up to yet.

We've moved from a shared monoculture where Walter Cronkite delivered the news to everyone, to fragmented realities where people choose their own version of truth. This isn't just a media problem—it's a leadership challenge that every woman managing teams, raising children, or building businesses needs to understand.

Steve's perspective on covering breaking news like 9/11 offers a masterclass in responsible leadership during crisis. He understood something that many leaders miss: your role isn't just to deliver information, but to balance truth with responsibility for how that information affects people. Whether you're delivering difficult news to your team or having tough conversations at home, this principle applies.

Why Gratitude Became His Secret Weapon

Perhaps the most powerful revelation in this conversation is Steve's discovery of gratitude as a daily mental health practice. This isn't feel-good fluff—this is a practical tool that kept him grounded through decades in an industry known for chewing people up and spitting them out.

Steve practices gratitude daily, not because his life is perfect, but because he understands something most people miss: gratitude is a choice that shapes your reality. When you're dealing with difficult employees, challenging children, or the constant pressure of leadership, gratitude becomes your reset button. It pulls you out of negative spirals and reminds you of what's actually working in your life.

The Social Media Wake-Up Call

As a mother of six, Aimee's conversation with Steve about social media's impact on young girls hits differently. Steve doesn't mince words: what we're seeing happen to young people, particularly girls, through social media is unprecedented and transformative in ways that aren't positive.

This isn't about being anti-technology—it's about recognizing that the tools we've given our children are rewiring their brains in real time. Steve's advice to get tablets out of kids' hands and prioritize real human connection isn't nostalgic thinking; it's recognizing that some things about human development haven't changed, even when technology has.

The implications for leaders are clear: if you're managing younger team members or raising children, you need to understand that their relationship with information, attention, and connection has been fundamentally altered. Your leadership approach needs to account for this reality.

Career Longevity in a Rapidly Changing World

Steve's 50-year career offers a blueprint for staying relevant that applies whether you're in media, technology, or any industry facing rapid change. His secret wasn't trying to stay young or chasing every trend—it was maintaining genuine curiosity while staying true to his authentic self.

The lesson for every woman building a career or business is this: reinvention doesn't mean becoming someone else. It means becoming a better, more evolved version of who you already are. Steve adapted to new technologies, co-hosts, and formats while never losing the core authenticity that made him effective in the first place.

The Power of Refusing to Become Irrelevant

One of Steve's most powerful pieces of advice is to refuse to become irrelevant. This isn't about desperately clinging to youth or trying to keep up with every trend. It's about maintaining the curiosity and adaptability that keep you growing regardless of your age or experience level.

For leaders, this means staying curious about your industry, your team, and the changing world around you. It means being willing to learn new skills, embrace new tools, and challenge your own assumptions. Most importantly, it means recognizing that experience is only valuable when combined with continued growth.

Building Your Own Media Strategy

Steve's insights about the fragmentation of media offer crucial guidance for leaders building their own platforms and messages. In a world where everyone chooses their own reality, your job isn't to appeal to everyone—it's to speak authentically to the people who need to hear your message.

Whether you're building a business, leading a team, or raising a family, the principle remains the same: authenticity beats performance every time. People can sense when you're performing a character versus when you're being genuinely yourself. In our fragmented media landscape, authenticity is what cuts through the noise.

This conversation with Steve Edwards isn't just about media—it's about how to build a sustainable, authentic life in a world that rewards performance over truth. His 50 years of experience offer a roadmap for career longevity, mental resilience, and staying true to yourself while adapting to change. The question isn't whether your industry will change—it's whether you'll maintain the curiosity, authenticity, and gratitude needed to thrive through that change.

quiz.title: What's Your Authenticity Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you show up authentically in your leadership and life, inspired by insights from broadcaster Steve Edwards' 50-year career.

questions.question: When you're facing a challenging situation at work, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Ask questions to understand all the angles and perspectives involved
Focus on communicating the truth while considering how it affects everyone
Find something to appreciate about the situation before diving into solutions
Look for ways to adapt or innovate rather than resist the challenge

questions.question: How do you typically handle moments when you feel pressure to be someone you're not?

options.text: Stay curious about why the pressure exists and what others really need
Stick to your values while finding ways to address others' concerns honestly
Practice gratitude for the opportunity to clarify who you really are
Use it as motivation to find new ways to be authentically yourself

questions.question: What energizes you most in your leadership role?

options.text: Discovering what makes people tick and connecting with their stories
Being someone others can count on for honest, thoughtful guidance
Helping others see the positive possibilities in difficult circumstances
Leading teams through change and helping them grow in the process

questions.question: When you think about your career longevity, what matters most to you?

options.text: Never losing your sense of wonder and willingness to learn new things
Being remembered as someone who could be trusted to tell the truth
Maintaining perspective and helping others do the same through ups and downs
Continuously evolving while staying true to your core identity

questions.question: How do you prefer to handle difficult conversations with your team?

options.text: Start by genuinely understanding their perspective before sharing yours
Balance honesty with care for their emotional wellbeing
Frame challenges within the context of growth and possibility
Focus on how you can all adapt and move forward together

questions.question: What's your relationship with change and uncertainty?

options.text: You see it as an opportunity to learn something new about yourself or others
You focus on being a steady, honest presence that others can rely on
You practice gratitude for the growth that often comes from uncertain times
You embrace it as a chance to reinvent and improve how you show up

results.title: The Curious Connector
results.description: You lead with genuine curiosity about people and situations, always asking the deeper questions that others miss. Your authenticity comes through in how you truly listen and adapt, making you naturally gifted at building trust and navigating change. You understand that staying relevant means staying curious, not performing a role. This episode will resonate with your approach to leadership and give you powerful insights about maintaining authenticity in an increasingly fragmented world.

results.title: The Steady Truth-Teller
results.description: You're the person others turn to when they need honest perspective delivered with care and wisdom. Your authenticity shines through your commitment to truth-telling balanced with deep responsibility for others' wellbeing. You understand that real leadership sometimes means protecting others from information overload while still being honest. Steve Edwards' insights about responsible communication during crisis will speak directly to your leadership style.

results.title: The Grateful Transformer
results.description: You've discovered that gratitude isn't just nice-to-have—it's your secret weapon for reframing challenges and maintaining perspective. Your authenticity comes from your ability to find meaning and growth in difficult situations, inspiring others to do the same. You naturally combat negative spirals with intentional positivity and genuine appreciation. This episode will validate your approach and give you new tools for using gratitude as a leadership superpower.

results.title: The Reinvention Artist
results.description: You're masterful at evolving and adapting while staying true to your core self, understanding that career longevity requires constant growth without losing your essence. Your authenticity shows up in how you embrace change and new challenges while maintaining your values and identity. You instinctively know when to pivot and when to stand firm. Steve's journey of reinventing himself over five decades will inspire your own path of authentic evolution.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Today's conversation is about longevity, reinvention, and the quiet discipline required to stay relevant across decades of cultural change. My guest is someone many of us grew up watching, a journalist whose voice helped shape the rhythm of morning television and whose career has spanned some of the most transformative chapters in modern media.

As co-anchor of Good Day LA, Steve Edwards didn't just deliver the news. He helped define how millions of people began their day. But what fascinates me most about Steve is not simply the length of his career. It's the mindset that sustained it. Before broadcasting, he was studying clinical psychology, already drawn to understanding human behavior. He built his career by saying yes to opportunity, by mastering the craft through repetition, and by adapting as the media landscape shifted from shared national narratives to the fragmented realities we navigate today.

He has experienced extraordinary success, public reinvention, professional disruption, and the kind of perspective that can only be earned through time. So today, this is not just a conversation about media. This is a conversation about resilience, identity, relevance, and what it truly means to build a life, not just a career, that lasts. Steve, it is a genuine honor to welcome you to The Manager.

**Steve:** Well, I'm very impressed with your guest. Is that me you're talking about?

**Aimee:** That would be you.

**Steve:** All right. Hi, Aimee. Thank you for having me here.

**Aimee:** I'm so glad you're here, Steve. So, so glad. So when you look back across your career, what do you believe most allowed you to endure, not just succeed briefly, but to remain relevant across generations?

**Steve:** Well, mainly enthusiasm and curiosity. And also the fun of it. I don't go into a very deep analysis of that part of it. I often thought of my career was much when I was a kid and I put on my clothes and go to the playground and I play all day and I play hard and I go in and lose and get knocked down, knock people down and all that stuff and came back the next day and just loved all of it. That I kind of looked at my career the same way. Just have fun. Well, start with always having fun.

Not every moment was fun. And of course, there are moments of such depth and seriousness that fun would never be a word you would apply to it. But even in the worst of times, I was glad that I was participating.

**Aimee:** So originally you were on the path toward clinical psychology. Looking back now, how much of your success has actually come from understanding people?

**Steve:** It's hard for me to say that, but what I would say is that a lot of my curiosity comes from why are you you? Why am I me? Why are people who are tremendously successful and reached all their dreams miserable? And why are there people who the brakes never went their way, but they have a smile on their face and they light up the room and they make other people feel better? Why are we at best, three steps from punching each other and five steps away from countries going to war? What is that all about? The human frailty. Why can't we get it right? We get a lot right, but why can't we all get along? It's impossible. So I want to know.

By the way, I don't have the answers at all. I still have the same questions. I'm not sure we'll ever know the answers, but it's always interesting to see the different perspectives.

**Aimee:** You have way more life experience than I do. And you've had such a different kind of vantage point, being behind the scenes in a newsroom versus us civilians. You've had a different vantage point.

**Steve:** Well, participating in what's going on, being a kind of a participant observer at the same time, does give you a take on things. And sometimes it's just, what do I say about this? How do we report on this? How do we handle this?

The thing that comes to mind all the time is 9-11. Being on the air 9-11, realizing that as things were happening, it isn't just a news story. And you also didn't know in the moment, how widespread is this? Is something happening in every American city at this very moment? Are there hundreds of thousands of people who are going to attack the United States at this moment? Or is it just what we know and dealing with the amount of information that we have, which wasn't much, and realizing that people listening to this, they're not only unsure, they're in a state of mild panic at least, buildings now dropping as we're on the air.

So the sense of such responsibility, trying to get it right, knowing you're not going to totally get it right, not wanting to scare people, not wanting to soften what was happening out there. And that's the time where, would you call that fun? No. No. But I was glad to be, I was happy. I felt, oh, there's some use in life for me in doing this at this moment.

**Aimee:** Yeah, that's really important to be there for all of us because we really trusted you. You were our guy. You're our morning guy. And this went down during your show.

**Steve:** It went down to the degree that I got on the air was already happening. I took over for Tony McKeown, who was doing the early morning. And I took over and Dorothy and Jill were there and things were happening. And one building went down. And then I see what I think is a video of the same building. And I'm saying, oh, this is horrible. We're showing you again. And Dorothy and Jill, I think, said to me, Steve, no, it's live. It's another building.

Oh my God, where are we going? What else is going to happen? We're hearing now about Washington. And I'm thinking, what about here? What about Chicago? What about Detroit? Is it happening everywhere? Is it happening around the world? You just didn't know the answer at all.

**Aimee:** Yeah. So scary. It was such a scary time for everyone in the whole world. I actually wasn't in town. I was in Costa Rica and my mom woke me up and said, Aimee, you have to come and see what's on the television. And I was, I think I was 21 or 22. And we just sat there with our mouths open, watching it live from the jungle thinking, is the world ending at this moment?

**Steve:** So part of that is, listen, Good Day LA was a funny, kooky morning show where things were said and done that weren't said and done in other places. It was kind of a reality show before reality shows. And there was a lot going on. But at some point, what mattered, the only thing that was important was getting the information out and getting it out correctly.

**Aimee:** And you guys did a good job of it and doing it in a friendly way that was relatable. You guys were so relatable.

**Steve:** Well, interestingly, the highest ratings we ever had were for the two or three or four or five weeks afterwards. And I realized it was neighborhood, neighborly, comfort food. Oh, good. They're there this morning. The world's in place. Those people are sitting there this morning. Good.

Because in a program, it's really a relationship with the audience. It's not so much, did you have a good guest? Did you do this right? Did you do that right? It's this relationship we have. And the relationship is such that they will forgive you for things. They'll be with you through a lot of things. But they're relying on you. When they need you, you're there.

**Aimee:** Yeah. We would turn you guys on in the morning and there you were. And you guys were relatable and you guys were predictable. We knew you guys were going to be there. You're going to make us laugh. You're going to give us the daily update so we can have conversations on the phone with other people and know what's going on in the world. We could grab those little bites so we could be in communion with all the other people that are experiencing the day.

It seems a little trivial thing to have a morning show, but it's such an important part of our society because now we live in this world of the screens tell us what the world is. And you guys lived in a time where the TV was kind of changing before our very eyes.

**Steve:** I mean, when I started on Good Day LA, the internet was going. Social media wasn't going yet. It was about to start. People got their information kind of in the old-fashioned way. You turned on the television. At 6:30, people turned on to watch the national news to get the news of the day, which is a very quaint notion now that anybody would wait until 6:30 to get the national news of the day.

It was in flux while we were on. You could tell. You could feel the change. Go to our website. I remember promoting the website. And I would joke on there saying, it's very important news. I'm not going to tell you, but go to our website. I mean, the world changed before your eyes.

**Aimee:** What year was it when you got started in the newsroom or on the radio?

**Steve:** My first job in radio was back in 2004. No, it was 1967. Yeah, 1967, when most people listening to this were not even a thought yet.

**Aimee:** I wasn't.

**Steve:** No, I walked into, I was in a PhD program at the University of Houston, studying to be a clinical psychologist. And I had a little bit of an epiphany of, I'm not sure I want to do this. What do I want to do? And I want to be in broadcasting. And so if you talk to any people I grew up with, there's no surprise. Ask my sister, because I sat with her hairbrush and do play by play in my bedroom and announce things, every broadcaster's ever done. 

I took a leave of absence, which I'm still on. And I just called every radio station. And it was a simple enough time that people, I got a couple of meetings and I ended up getting a job. And I went from a guy with a fantasy of being in radio to having a six hour radio show with playing music and news. And I was doing everything. And it was a tremendous education. I think I had an audience of six. On a good day, three on a small day. But I didn't know that.

And in a relatively brief period of time, again, because of enthusiasm and curiosity and playing, going to the playground, I ended up being the morning man, the sports director, the sales manager. And I did that for about 11 or 12 months. And I thought, now I'm ready. And there was a station that I listened to, and it was the big talk news station. And that's where I wanted to go.

And I called them and I got the guy who was the program director, who was also on the air. And I said, I want to come to work at your station. And he said to me, well, what's your broadcast background? I said, honestly, a year. And he said, well, this is veteran award-winning 25-year veterans. And what's your educational background? I said, well, I'm actually still kind of in a program for PhD at University of Houston. He said, well, come on in. 

So if people ever ask me, what did your education ever do for you? Very overtly and clearly, it got me that meeting that I wouldn't have gotten, probably. And I went, and we had lunch, and then we had dinner, and then we had drinks, and I had a career after that.

It's a wonderful story about the benefits of education. And by the way, let me give you a perspective on education. It never ends. I'm learning more now than I ever did in college. At this late stage in my life, I'm still learning more. There is a kind of small thrill you get from learning.

**Aimee:** What are you studying? What are you studying now, Steve?

**Steve:** Everything. And because of, I go back and forth on the Internet. Is it plus plus for humanity or not? And I don't have an answer for that because it's also awful and it spreads misinformation and hatred and all of that. But boy, all the knowledge of the world is at our fingertips. You can get educated in everything and it takes no effort.

I can remember when I was in radio and I had a show where I would interview people. I would go to the library. And I'd sit there and I'd go through something called a periodic guide of literature. And it would show you, oh, there's an interview with so-and-so in this magazine. And then you get the thing and you make the points and whatever. And you're there for seven hours for an interview that's going to last seven minutes. Now you go, thank you. I got it all. There's no excuse for anybody not to learn.

**Aimee:** Oh, I love learning. I walked into my son's room and he said, oh, I've been studying Japanese. I said, really? He said, yeah, I have a 200 day streak on Duolingo. This kid is 11 years old. So he's been teaching himself Japanese without even telling me for 200 days. I didn't even notice.

**Steve:** Wow. Yeah. And that's the great part of this. Do you want to know something about the Etruscan civilization? Somebody said, what's the Etruscan? Okay. In five minutes, you will know as much as somebody who goes to Cambridge University to learn about it, because it's all right there.

**Aimee:** I know. I got a degree in art history, and now I can just look everything up. I can just ask ChatGPT.

**Steve:** Which is phenomenal. It is phenomenal, isn't it? It's almost lazy. You just have to work to learn it. Now you don't have to work so hard.

**Aimee:** You don't have to worry about forgetting anything anymore, though. It's all there.

**Steve:** That's true.

**Aimee:** So very few careers last. What separates the people who sustain relevance from those who fade?

**Steve:** Well, first of all, luck. Luck has to do with it. I fall back on, again, on enthusiasm and curiosity and feeling the playfulness of a career, even when it's serious. Wanting to be there. I always felt I was doing my avocation as vocation. It's something that I would have done for nothing, much less have a professional career, earning a living at it. For me, that would be my answer.

And the fact that I'm interested in everything so that there was lots of slots I could always fill. I was a sportscaster. I was the weather guy. I was the entertainment guy. I did Entertainment Tonight for a year. I did sports talk on KBC radio for a bunch of years. I did the weather. It's nothing I really ever wanted to do, but I did the weather.

I basically always thought of myself as a talk show host slash news junkie. That would be the combination because I realized early in my career when I went to television and I was a news anchorman in Houston that I found it boring. And because what it was, was reading copy, either written by somebody else or by me, and just reading what you already know is on the page. And I discovered something about myself. I'm more excited if I don't know quite what is on the page or what's around the corner. This is why I love breaking news and live television and things going on and a certain degree of comfort in chaos.

**Aimee:** I get that. I understand that. I'm a mother, so I have comfort in chaos in my daily life. I get that all the way.

**Steve:** Well, and also, and I'm going to try not to interview you. It's a reflex for me.

**Aimee:** Go ahead, Steve.

**Steve:** It's very hard for me not to. This whole thing about chaos of motherhood, first of all, you have 17 children. How many children do you have?

**Aimee:** Six.

**Steve:** Six. You have six children. And you're doing this and you have a book and you have a business and you're still expanding your horizons. And not only do you want to do it for you, you want it to have a positive impact on so many other people. So it's not just me, me, me. Although it's got to be a healthy part of me, me, me in it because you can't do for others if you're not doing for yourself.

This idea you have, which is so simple, so obvious, and yet people miss it. And that is so much what you do as a mother and running a house and a family requires so many skills, so much decision-making that as a society, we don't value it. So you don't get credit for doing some stuff that's not easy at all. And the other thing is it never ends. If you have a career, you go home. The career's over for the day. Home never ends. Kids never end. It never, ever ends.

**Aimee:** No, you don't. There are no vacations. You just move locations. So let me turn it on you, okay? How do you always have the energy? I know you're a human being. I know that there are times when I'm sure you go off in a corner. But what do you do once in a while to look at the pile you have in front of you and think, I can't do it. I can't do it. What do you do?

**Steve:** I'm really good at compartmentalizing my life. So when I'm at home, I really try to just put my mama hat on and turn off, usually I'm not writing my book. I'm not thinking about my podcast. I'm not thinking about work. I'll just be home. Because I do have a three-year-old and a five-year-old still and so when I'm home, it's they need me. They need my attention. They need me to sit on the ground and build Magnetile towers with them. And you have to go into their world. They're not going into yours.

Yeah. It's not fair to expect them to be able to come into my world. They're babies. But I can go into their world for a few minutes or for a few hours a day. I can, and I enjoy it. I want to color with my daughter. She found these little golden tattoos. And so now every time we get together, she's mama, put another one on my hand. So it's always some little sweet thing that we're doing and they to help me cook. So we'll cook dinner together. And when I'm there, I'm there.

And when I'm here and I'm at the office and I have my podcaster producer hat on, that's what I'm doing. And I really will put my phone on, do not disturb. And there are other big people that can handle their problems for the few hours that I'm focusing and concentrating on what I'm doing. I don't believe in multitasking with different hats on. I think I can keep one hat on at a time, stay focused for a few hours on that kind of work. And that keeps me kind of sane.

**Aimee:** How do you sleep?

**Steve:** Very well. I am a big sleeper. Sleep is huge to me. It's at least eight hours a night. And my husband, the first time he took me away on a trip before we got married, he cracked up because I have my sleeping socks, my sleeping mask, my earplugs. And he laughed at me. He was, are you serious? 

**Aimee:** Well, wait, wait, where's your white noise machine?

**Steve:** I didn't bring it with me. But yeah, he's oh, whoa, this chick really takes sleep seriously. But I do. I do. I need eight hours of sleep for real every day. And some people don't need sleep. I need sleep. I love sleep. Sleep is good.

**Aimee:** Well, but you have a pretty big battery to charge with everything you're doing.

**Steve:** Yeah. Yes, I do. Yeah. Sleep's always been kind of it's non-negotiable. How do you handle anxiety?

**Aimee:** I'm not a very anxious person. I don't get very anxious. I do get scared sometimes. I do. I would say it's not really anxiety for me, but I do have self-doubt. And a lot of times when I'm scared or have self-doubt, as I'm getting older, I actually push into it. So things I'm afraid of, I actually realize those are the things I need to do. It's kind of thinking there's a monster under the bed. You look under the bed and it's not really there. You're not afraid of it anymore. And that's sort of life.

**Steve:** There's a phrase you can use for that psychological phrase. You're being counterphobic. Counterphobic.

**Aimee:** Counterphobic. I it. Yeah. So that's sort of what I've learned. And my next book that I'm writing right now kind of really gets into that shadow work and how we can use our fear and the things, the emotions that we're running away from and the problems we're running away from in our life to help us actually meet and exceed our potential. You write and talk much about the wound.

**Steve:** the wound. Yeah.

**Aimee:** Yeah. In the book, the second book really gets into the wound even more, honestly. It was one of the big questions that people were, okay, tell me more about the wound. And the wound is, in the bigger sense, the wound is the patriarchal wound that was left on the female psyche from millennia of patriarchal rule that told women they were less than, that told women that what they did wasn't important. That women's work wasn't really considered high level, high skilled.

That's really one of the things that I wanted to, I wanted to change the narrative on that with my book, with the manager. It was, let's change the narrative on this. Women, the work we do with our families, with our husbands, with our children, this is highly skilled management organization. This is emotional IQ. This is some seriously highly skilled work that nature has actually helped us evolve to do. And let's own that. The fact that we are doing this work and that is skilled labor and that it's really a masterclass in leadership as a whole. So for any woman who's out there who feels she isn't a leader, if you're a mom, you know how to lead. I'm giving you permission. These skills really translate well into the working world. Nobody gives it too much credit yet.

But when you're skilled at all those other things, hey, being in an office, working with people, that's easy if they're not kids. My eyes really lit up in 2020, my husband got sick and I had to step in and run our two IT companies while he was unwell. And I had just given birth to Indy. She was born in 2020. And in 21, I got pregnant with Paul. So I ran these companies for quite a few years. And as I was running the home and running all these kids, all of a sudden I sort of realized at some point that there was just a lot of crossover in the capital management, people management, and systems management that go into both a household and in running a company.

And so anytime I felt I couldn't do it, I just went back to how am I doing this at home? What are my standard operating procedures in my home. I have lots of strategic action plans in my home. If I'm going to have a home birth, that's a strategic action plan. Let me outline it. This, this, this, and this, who's going to be there? Where are we going to do it? What are the logistics of it? Do I have all the equipment? This is a strategic action plan. You're planning to buy a house. That's a strategic action plan. You can put that together step by step. You know exactly how you're going to get there.

Where your spoons go in your kitchen is a standard operating procedure, SOP. And in business, we use SOPs for everything from how accounting receives purchase orders. I mean, there's an SOP for absolutely everything in business. And in our homes, it's very much the same. Just no one ever taught us the words SOP. So it's about reclaiming the language, I think, of business in the home so that we can take credit for the work that we've been doing there all along.

And of course, you just dropped in quietly home birth, which as if, oh, yeah, all that home birth thing. What kind of reaction did you get from your family when you decided to do that?

**Steve:** So, my mom has got a Ph.D. in pharmacology. She did her internship at Cedars-Sinai and I was born at Cedars and she was, what? Why? Why would you want to do that? So I had already read five books on it and I was very low risk. I was very young. Well, I was not too young, 30, right in the zone of safety. And I'd read a lot of books that actually having a home birth was less risky than having a hospital birth in America. And that midwifery model of care is actually the standard in socialized medicine countries Sweden and England and pretty much all of Europe. And I thought, well, let me see if I can find a midwife.

There were really, really had been wiped out, Steve. I don't know if you know this, but during the 90s, midwifery was banned in California. And midwives were getting locked up for assisting women with their births at home.

**Aimee:** Didn't know that. What is the status now?

**Steve:** Now it is legal. I think it became legal again in 1999. But actually, my first midwife, Anne Summers, actually did get locked up in the 90s for being a midwife.

**Aimee:** Really?

**Steve:** Yeah. So we've come a long way. But it was in 2009. It was kind of hard to find a midwife because there had been kind of a collapse of midwifery in the state of California. There are so many fabulous midwives now. I've definitely had a resurgence. And the midwife I used for my last two babies is Lindsay Miles. And she's total boss and celebrity midwife and has an amazing career. And she's a fabulous human being and a very good friend of mine.

But I'd love to see the resurgence of home birth, if it's safe, if you're low risk. And I always tell women, if it scares you, don't do it. However you feel about birth, whatever feels it's going to be the safest option for you is the safest option.

**Aimee:** People, if they didn't know you at all, say, oh, crunchy granola, right?

**Steve:** A little bit. A little bit, but crunchy granola, business driven orientation. Those things usually don't go hand in hand.

**Aimee:** No, they don't. And Steve, I'm also a yogi. I've been doing yoga for 17 years. Yeah, no, it's funny. ChatGPT actually told me that I am a spiritualpreneur is what it told me. And I thought, okay. All right.

**Steve:** Listen, also people have to learn how to say the word midwifery. It just flows out of your mouth. Midwifery.

**Aimee:** I know it does, because I've actually had five babies at home, Steve.

**Steve:** Wow. Any problems?

**Aimee:** None. No. It actually got easier every single time.

**Steve:** Okay. And you live near a hospital?

**Aimee:** Yeah. Just in case. Just in case.

**Steve:** Just in case. Yep. Just in case.

**Aimee:** Yeah. And you're constantly being monitored, but I don't know. I just, you get to a place where you trust your body too, especially when you've had, I feel once you've had two home births, your body really knows what it's doing. And birth is really just a biological function at that point. You just trust it.

**Steve:** Let me ask a crude man question. Are you done?

**Aimee:** I wish I could have another baby, but we're aging out. I would totally have one more if I could, but I think I've aged out.

**Steve:** Okay. All right. You asked me something. So let's see. Did you always know how to adapt or did adaptation teach you?

**Aimee:** I think it's probably a combination of two things. The adaptation comes from, again, the fun of wanting to try stuff. Even being slightly, let's use this phrase again, slightly counterphobic. Am I afraid to do that? Let's do that. People ask me, did you ever get nervous when you're on the air? And I say, I would get nervous the first time I ever did anything for the leading up to it and the first two minutes of it. And that's it, because now you're doing it.

The fear of something happening is much worse than the event itself quite often. So that certainly was a big part of it. And I think it's always key to say, don't say no. Don't say no. You brought this up in the introduction. Don't say no. I wanted to do everything when I started, whatever it was. I want to try that job and this job. And I love being on the air. And I can't believe everybody wanted to be on the air. Now I'm on the air.

All right. I never wanted to forget in the endless career that I had the thrill of, wait a minute, I'm doing this. Once in a while I forget because business and taking care of things and looking at the ratings and sales and all that stuff. Wait a minute. Wait a second. Let's remember the thrill of this. Let's remember that, wow, I'm still doing this. Pinch myself. I'm still doing this. And I'd often want to remind people of that. Because sometimes work is work. You can be in the most heightened place, but if you're doing it all the time, it's just what you're doing. And somebody else comes along and says, wow, you get to do that. Reminds you, oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

**Steve:** Oh, yeah, Steve. Well, was there ever a moment when the version of success you were chasing quietly changed?

**Aimee:** Well, yes. I never intended to be thought of as a news person. As I said, I started in music radio for a little while, and then in talk radio, which was very news oriented, but I always talking about news and talking about the issues, not being the newscaster. And the perfect amalgamation of that for me was doing Good Day LA because I got to do all of it all the time.

So if you notice a lot of newscasters, don't talk people. And one of the things that I learned, remember when I first started doing television news, before I'd go on, I'd say, good evening. Good evening. And that was back in the days of the stern, serious newscasters. Good evening. Good evening. So every time I was on, I was slightly somebody else until I realized, stop it. Find your voice. You know your voice. I don't have to find it. I'm faking it when I go in. And what happens with a lot of people, they fake it so much that they think that's how they talk now. Good evening.

I love when I see a 24-year-old kid on TV. My colleague, I said, you never say colleague in your life. Why are you saying that? Because you're putting on adult clothing and you're pretending.

Another observation. Here's another observation I have. When I first started, we all wanted to seem older. Not anymore. Everybody wants to seem younger. Everybody wants to be back then. You want to be perceived as someone with a little maturity, some age on you and experience on you. Now, if you look at pretty much any person in the news, with very few exceptions, you look at every local news station and then you look at the social media and everybody's cute and adorable and funny and doing silly things. That would be unimaginable when I got into the business. People wouldn't present themselves that way unless they were doing some kind of goofy show, but not if they're doing something attached to news. That's a big change.

**Steve:** Yeah. Tell me more about that time, Steve.

**Aimee:** Back then, first of all, it had to do with a lot of the degree of what was being put on the air and disseminated was determined by scarcity. I mean, you only had a couple of places. So that when I watched the news, the name that would always come up is Walter Cronkite. So if you're watching Walter Cronkite, he's on for half an hour, which means 24 minutes, and 25 million or 30 million people would be watching him every night when the population of the country was about 190 million.

Now the number one primetime newscast, the 6:30 newscast, gets 8 million in a country of 340 million people. So there weren't a lot of places to go. So we were living in a monoculture where people were sharing the same experience. Because either you were watching Walter Cronkite or the other two national newscasts, or you were hearing about it.

So that when Walter Cronkite said, relative to the Vietnam War, the best we can hope for now is a stalemate. America knew it's time for us to get out. Because there's nobody that now. There's nobody that now.

Also, because there were so few places to get news, the newscasters, the newscasts, they weren't chasing an audience. In other words, they weren't doing now, it's kind of, what do people want to hear? What do they want to hear? And we'll go with that. It's true of the important national newscasts, but more so where most people consume their news, which is on cable news, on streaming, and they're playing what they think you want.

Back then, you had, it was kind of elitist. You had these elitist news guys, the gatekeepers, thinking, what is important for America to hear? Not, hey, what do they want to hear? The Kardashians never would have been on a newscast. You would have never heard of them.

When Elvis Presley died, what a story, right? Elvis Presley died. He was the second or third story on the CBS Evening News about a minute, 10 seconds. That was it. Wow.
